articles.title: Light Twists Like a Tornado! New Discovery Could Supercharge Computers
articles.summary: Scientists have created tiny 'optical tornadoes' inside a special material. These swirling light beams could make future computers and internet connections much faster. The team used liquid crystals to trap light and make it spin. This is the first time such light has been made in a stable, low-energy state. The discovery could lead to simpler, cheaper devices for quantum communication.

articles.title: Giant Underground Cave Found on Venus! Bigger Than Any on Earth
articles.summary: Scientists discovered a huge lava tube on Venus by re-examining old radar images from the 1990s. The tube is at least 375 meters deep and could be up to 1 kilometer wide—larger than any lava tube on Earth or Mars. It was found near a volcano called Nyx Mons. The discovery will help future missions like NASA's VERITAS and ESA's EnVision explore Venus in more detail.

articles.title: Teacher and Students Build Life-Size Replica of One of the First Computers
articles.summary: Tom Burick, a teacher at a school for students with autism, led his class in building a full-scale replica of ENIAC, one of the world's first electronic computers. The project celebrates the 80th anniversary of ENIAC's construction. Burick, who has dyscalculia, uses hands-on projects to help his neurodivergent students learn. The replica matches the original computer's appearance before it was disassembled in the 1950s.
